en.extract:
The Zenaida dove (Zenaida aurita) is a member of the bird family Columbidae, which includes doves and pigeons. It is the national bird of Anguilla, where it is locally referred to as "turtle dove".


Description
The Zenaida dove is approximately 28–30 cm (11–12 in) in length. It looks very similar to the mourning dove, but is smaller in size, has a shorter, more rounded tail, and is a bit more darkly colored. It is also distinguished from the mourning dove by showing white on the trailing edge of its wings while in flight. The mourning dove does not have the white trailing edge.


Breeding
It lays two white eggs on a flimsy platform, built on a tree or shrub. It also nests in rock crevices, and on grassy vegetation if no predators are present. It has been recorded that some birds have up to 4 broods per year.  Eggs take approximately two weeks to hatch, and the young chicks typically fledge after only two weeks in the nest. Parents feed the young pigeon's milk, a nutrient rich substance regurgitated from its crop.


Range, habitat and behaviour

The Zenaida dove breeds throughout the Caribbean and the tip of the Yucatán Peninsula, an example habitat being in the Petenes mangroves. It was reported by John James Audubon to breed in the Florida Keys, but there are only three verifiable records from Florida. It is found in a variety of open and semi-open habitats. Its mournful cooOOoo-coo-coo-coo call is similar to the call of a mourning dove, but faster in pace.
The bird is resident and abundant over much of its range. Zenaida doves are commonly hunted as a game bird.


Diet
These birds forage on the ground, mainly eating grains and seeds, sometimes also on insects. Zenaida doves frequently feed close to water. They often swallow fine gravel to assist with digestion, and will also ingest salt from mineral rich soils or livestock salt licks. It is thought the salt aids in egg formation and/or production of pigeon milk.


References

"National Geographic"  Field Guide to the Birds of North America ISBN 0-7922-6877-6
Handbook of the Birds of the World Vol 4,  Josep del Hoyo editor, ISBN 84-87334-22-9
World Wildlife Fund. 2010. Petenes mangroves. eds. Mark McGinley, C.Michael Hogan & C. Cleveland. Encyclopedia of Earth. National Council for Science and the Environment. Washington DC


External links
 Media related to Zenaida aurita at Wikimedia Commons

The '''Zenaida dove''' ('''''Zenaida aurita''''') is a member of the [[bird]] family [[Columbidae]],<ref>{{Cite web |title=Pigeons and Doves (Family Columbidae) |url=https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/2715-Columbidae |access-date=2024-03-22 |website=iNaturalist |language=en}}</ref> which includes [[dove]]s and pigeons. It is the [[national bird]] of [[Anguilla]], where it is locally referred to as "[[Turtur|turtle dove]]".

==Description==
The Zenaida dove is approximately {{convert|28|-|30|cm|in|abbr=on}} in length. It looks very similar to the [[mourning dove]], but is smaller in size, has a shorter, more rounded tail, and is a bit more darkly colored. It is also distinguished from the mourning dove by showing white on the trailing edge of its wings while in flight. The mourning dove does not have the white trailing edge.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Wiley |first=James |date=2023-12-19 |title=Ecology and Behavior of the Zenaida Dove |url=https://digitalcommons.usf.edu/ornitologia_neotropical/vol2/iss2/1 |journal=Ornitología Neotropical |volume=2 |issue=2 |pages=53–58}}</ref>

==Breeding==
It lays two white eggs on a flimsy platform, built on a tree or shrub.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2013-04-19 |title=The Turtle Dove - My Anguilla Experience {{!}} My Anguilla Experience |url=https://www.myanguillaexperience.com/anguillas-national-bird-the-turtle-dove/,%20https://www.myanguillaexperience.com/anguillas-national-bird-the-turtle-dove/ |access-date=2024-03-22 |website=www.myanguillaexperience.com |language=en-US }}{{Dead link|date=August 2025 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }}</ref> It also nests in rock crevices, and on grassy vegetation if no predators are present. It has been recorded that some birds have up to 4 broods per year.  Eggs take approximately two weeks to hatch, and the young chicks typically fledge after only two weeks in the nest. Parents feed the young [[crop milk|pigeon's milk]], a nutrient rich substance [[Regurgitation (digestion)|regurgitated]] from its [[crop (anatomy)|crop]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Gillespie |first1=Meagan J. |last2=Haring |first2=Volker R. |last3=McColl |first3=Kenneth A. |last4=Monaghan |first4=Paul |last5=Donald |first5=John A. |last6=Nicholas |first6=Kevin R. |last7=Moore |first7=Robert J. |last8=Crowley |first8=Tamsyn M. |date=2011-09-19 |title=Histological and global gene expression analysis of the 'lactating' pigeon crop |journal=BMC Genomics |volume=12 |article-number=452 |doi=10.1186/1471-2164-12-452 |doi-access=free |issn=1471-2164 |pmc=3191541 |pmid=21929790}}</ref>
{{Phylogeny/Zenaida}}

==Range, habitat and behaviour==
[[File:Zenaida aurita aurita MHNT.ZOO.2010.11.228.15.jpg|thumb|''Zenaida aurita aurita'' - [[MHNT]]]]
The Zenaida dove breeds throughout the [[Caribbean]]<ref name="DRNA">{{cite web |title=Bosque Estatal Monte Choca |url=http://www.drna.pr.gov/wp-content/uploads/2015/04/El-Bosque-Estatal-Monte-Choca.pdf |website=DRNA |publisher=Puerto Rico Department of Natural Resources |language=Spanish}}</ref> and the tip of the [[Yucatán Peninsula]], an example habitat being in the Petenes mangroves. It was reported by [[John James Audubon]] to breed in the [[Florida Keys]], but there are only three verifiable records from [[Florida]]. It is found in a variety of open and semi-open habitats. Its mournful ''{{not a typo|cooOOoo-coo-coo-coo}}'' call is similar to the call of a mourning dove, but faster in pace.<ref name=":0" />

The bird is resident and abundant over much of its range. Zenaida doves are commonly hunted as a game bird.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Houvener |first=Daniel |title=Zenaida auriculata (eared dove) |url=https://animaldiversity.org/accounts/Zenaida_auriculata/ |access-date=2025-07-23 |website=Animal Diversity Web |language=en}}</ref>

==Diet==
These birds forage on the ground, mainly eating [[Caryopsis|grains]] and [[seed]]s, sometimes also on [[insect]]s. Zenaida doves frequently feed close to water. They often swallow fine [[gravel]] to assist with [[digestion]], and will also ingest salt from mineral rich soils or livestock salt licks. It is thought the salt aids in egg formation and/or production of pigeon milk.<ref name=":0" />[[File:Zenaida Dove 20240308 163624.jpg|thumb|The national bird of Anguilla]]

zh.extract:
鳴哀鴿（學名：Zenaida aurita）是鳩鴿科的一種鳥類。它們是安圭拉的國鳥，分佈在加勒比海地區及尤卡坦半島。有指它們在佛羅里達礁島群繁殖，但在科羅拉多州經證實的卻只有三次紀錄。它們會在樹或灌木上築巢，每次會生兩顆鳥蛋。若沒有掠食者，它們也會在石縫間及植被上築巢。一些鳴哀鴿每年可以生4胎。鳥蛋要2星期才孵化，幼鳥一般在2星期大就會換羽。雙親會以反芻物來餵哺雛鳥。
鳴哀鴿是留鳥，在其分佈地十分普遍。它們長約28-30厘米。它們的外觀很像哀鴿，但體型較為細小，尾巴較短及圓，顏色也較深。它們的雙翼上有一些白邊。
鳴哀鴿棲息在廣闊及半開放的環境。它們的叫聲很像哀鴿，但稍為快速。
鳴哀鴿會在地上覓食，主要吃殼物及種子，有時也會吃昆蟲。它們一般會到水邊的地方覓食。它們經常吞下小石來幫助消化，也會從土壤或鹽鹼地攝取鹽份。相信鹽份可以幫助形成鳥蛋或生產鴿乳。


參考

"National Geographic"  Field Guide to the Birds of North America ISBN 0-7922-6877-6
Handbook of the Birds of the World Vol 4,  Josep del Hoyo editor, ISBN 84-87334-22-9
